entities.name: ParkingFloor
entities.responsibility: Manages parking spots on one floor

entities.name: ParkingLot
entities.responsibility: Main coordinator for parking and exit operations

entities.name: FeeCalculationStrategy
entities.responsibility: Calculates parking fee using pluggable pricing rules

steps.explanation: Driver requests parking for a car.
Parking lot asks floors to find a compatible available spot.
Parking floor checks whether the spot supports this vehicle type and is free.
The selected spot stores the vehicle and becomes occupied.
Parking lot generates a ticket with vehicle, spot and entry time.
Driver exits using the parking ticket.
Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy.
The occupied spot is freed after payment.

patterns.name: Singleton
patterns.reason: Only one central parking lot manager should coordinate all parking operations.

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.reason: Pricing rules can change without modifying ParkingLot.

dryRuns.title: Example Dry Run
steps: Car KA-01 enters the parking lot.
ParkingLot searches floors for an available CAR spot.
On exit, fee is calculated and C1 becomes free.

tradeoffs: Simple manager class vs multiple services
Nearest spot allocation vs first available spot
Fixed fee calculation vs strategy-based fee calculation
mistakesToAvoid: Putting all logic inside one ParkingLot class
Not freeing the spot on exit
constraints: Keep the core workflow deterministic and testable.
Keep state changes explicit and easy to validate.
Design should support new rules without rewriting existing classes.

interfaces.name: FeeCalculationStrategy
interfaces.responsibility: Calculates parking fee using pluggable pricing rules

publicMethods.purpose: Manages parking spots on one floor

dataStructures: Priority queue or ordered list for nearest available spots.
edgeCases: Reject unsupported input before mutating state.
Validate state before allowing an operation.
errorHandling: Return domain-specific errors for invalid state.
Keep failure messages actionable for callers.
concurrencyConcerns: Prevent two gates from reserving the same spot.
Make ticket creation idempotent for retries.

scenes.subtitle: medium LLD Interview Design

rightPanel.heading: Interview Goal
rightPanel.body: Design clean classes, interfaces, object flows, edge cases, tests, and trade-offs for Design a Parking Lot.

scenes.title: Problem Statement
cards: Model Design a Parking Lot with clear domain entities.
Support support multiple parking floors and support different vehicle types.
Keep the design extensible for new rules, states, and workflows.

rightPanel.heading: Core Objects
bullets: Vehicle: Base class for all vehicles
Car: Represents a car vehicle
ParkingFloor: Manages parking spots on one floor
ParkingSpot: Represents one parking slot
ParkingTicket: Stores parking session details
ParkingLot: Main coordinator for parking and exit operations
rightPanel.interviewNote: Good LLD separates object responsibilities before adding methods.

scenes.title: Object Collaboration Flow

steps.explanation: Driver requests parking for a car.
Parking lot asks floors to find a compatible available spot.
Parking floor checks whether the spot supports this vehicle type and is free.
The selected spot stores the vehicle and becomes occupied.
Parking lot generates a ticket with vehicle, spot and entry time.

rightPanel.heading: Park Vehicle Flow
bullets: Driver calls ParkingLot.parkVehicle(car)
ParkingLot calls ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle)
ParkingFloor calls ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle)
ParkingSpot calls ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle)
ParkingLot calls ParkingTicket.createTicket(vehicle, spot)
rightPanel.interviewNote: A collaboration flow proves that the class model is executable.

scenes.narration: Park Vehicle Flow Driver calls ParkingLot.parkVehicle(car) ParkingLot calls ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle) ParkingFloor calls ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle) ParkingSpot calls ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle) ParkingLot calls ParkingTicket.createTicket(vehicle, spot) A collaboration flow proves that the class model is executable.

scenes.title: Exit Vehicle Flow

steps.explanation: Driver exits using the parking ticket.
Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy.
The occupied spot is freed after payment.

rightPanel.heading: Exit Vehicle Flow
bullets: Driver exits using the parking ticket.
Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy.
The occupied spot is freed after payment.
rightPanel.interviewNote: Sequence diagrams expose missing dependencies and invalid state changes.

scenes.narration: Exit Vehicle Flow Driver exits using the parking ticket. Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy. The occupied spot is freed after payment. Sequence diagrams expose missing dependencies and invalid state changes.

scenes.title: State Transitions

rightPanel.heading: State Machine
bullets: SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved: SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved on entry.
SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied: SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied after vehicle parks.
SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable: SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable after paid exit.
rightPanel.interviewNote: Explicit states prevent invalid operations from slipping into code.

scenes.narration: State Machine SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved: SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved on entry. SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied: SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied after vehicle parks. SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable: SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable after paid exit. Explicit states prevent invalid operations from slipping into code.

voiceoverScript:
## Scene 1: Title (3s)
Interview Goal Design clean classes, interfaces, object flows, edge cases, tests, and trade-offs for Design a Parking Lot.

## Scene 2: Problem Statement (5s)
Problem Framing Model Design a Parking Lot with clear domain entities. Support support multiple parking floors and support different vehicle types. Keep the design extensible for new rules, states, and workflows. State scope, assumptions, and key workflows before drawing classes.

## Scene 3: Functional Requirements (5s)
Functional Requirements Support multiple parking floors Support different vehicle types Assign nearest available parking spot Generate parking ticket Calculate parking fee on exit Requirements become methods, state transitions, and tests.

## Scene 4: Constraints and Assumptions (5s)
Constraints Keep the core workflow deterministic and testable. Keep state changes explicit and easy to validate. Design should support new rules without rewriting existing classes. Constraints decide whether the design needs locks, strategies, or repositories.

## Scene 5: Core Objects and Classes (5.5s)
Core Objects Vehicle: Base class for all vehicles Car: Represents a car vehicle ParkingFloor: Manages parking spots on one floor ParkingSpot: Represents one parking slot ParkingTicket: Stores parking session details ParkingLot: Main coordinator for parking and exit operations Good LLD separates object responsibilities before adding methods.

## Scene 6: Class Diagram (7s)
Class Diagram ParkingLot coordinates the main workflow. Vehicle anchors the model. Composition owns object lifetime; dependencies express collaboration. Long explanations stay in the panel so the diagram remains clean. Use the diagram to prove ownership and collaboration, not to dump text.

## Scene 7: Interface Design (5.5s)
Interface Contracts FeeCalculationStrategy: Calculates parking fee using pluggable pricing rules Use interfaces only where behavior genuinely varies.

## Scene 8: Public Method Contracts (6.2s)
Public Methods Vehicle.getVehicleType(): Base class for all vehicles Vehicle.getVehicleNumber(): Base class for all vehicles ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle): Manages parking spots on one floor ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle): Represents one parking slot ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle): Represents one parking slot Method contracts turn requirements into testable behavior.

## Scene 9: Object Collaboration Flow (6s)
Park Vehicle Flow Driver calls ParkingLot.parkVehicle(car) ParkingLot calls ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle) ParkingFloor calls ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle) ParkingSpot calls ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle) ParkingLot calls ParkingTicket.createTicket(vehicle, spot) A collaboration flow proves that the class model is executable.

## Scene 10: Exit Vehicle Flow (6.2s)
Exit Vehicle Flow Driver exits using the parking ticket. Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy. The occupied spot is freed after payment. Sequence diagrams expose missing dependencies and invalid state changes.

## Scene 11: State Transitions (6s)
State Machine SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved: SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved on entry. SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied: SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied after vehicle parks. SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable: SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable after paid exit. Explicit states prevent invalid operations from slipping into code.

## Scene 12: Design Pattern Choice (5s)
Singleton Used in ParkingLot Only one central parking lot manager should coordinate all parking operations. Name a pattern only when it solves a concrete design pressure.

## Scene 13: Data Structures Used (5s)
Data Structures Map floorId to ParkingFloor. Priority queue or ordered list for nearest available spots. Map ticketId to active ParkingTicket. Data structures should justify time complexity and state ownership.

## Scene 14: Edge Cases (6s)
Edge Cases Reject unsupported input before mutating state. Validate availability before assigning resources. Validate state before allowing an operation. Edge cases reveal whether the design is robust or just happy-path.

## Scene 15: Error Handling (5.5s)
Error Handling Return domain-specific errors for invalid state. Release temporary resources on failure. Keep failure messages actionable for callers. Handle failure before mutating shared or persistent state.

## Scene 16: Concurrency and Thread Safety (6s)
Concurrency Lock spot assignment per floor. Prevent two gates from reserving the same spot. Make ticket creation idempotent for retries. Interviewers often push LLD designs with concurrent requests.

## Scene 17: Code Skeleton: Vehicle.java (8s)
Code Skeleton Show the core fields and method contracts. Keep behavior near the object that owns the state. Avoid turning the coordinator into a catch-all class. Code skeletons should prove design shape, not become full production code.

## Scene 18: Unit Test Cases (6s)
Tests as Design Proof Happy path creates the expected state changes. Invalid input is rejected without mutation. Concurrent requests do not allocate the same resource twice. Tests prove the class responsibilities and edge cases are clear.

## Scene 19: Trade-offs (5.5s)
Trade-offs Simple manager class vs multiple services Nearest spot allocation vs first available spot Fixed fee calculation vs strategy-based fee calculation In-memory design vs persistent storage Strong candidates explain why they did not over-engineer.

## Scene 20: Interview Pitfalls (5s)
Mistakes to Avoid Putting all logic inside one ParkingLot class Ignoring vehicle type compatibility Not freeing the spot on exit Skipping sequence flow explanation Calling out pitfalls shows you understand failure modes.

## Scene 21: Final LLD Summary (5s)
Final Summary ParkingLot coordinates core use cases. Vehicle, Car, ParkingFloor carry primary state. Park Vehicle Flow explains object collaboration. Edge cases, tests, and trade-offs make the design interview-ready. End by showing how the design can evolve without changing everything.

## Scene 22: End Screen (2.6s)
Next Steps Add persistence and external API boundaries. Add metrics and operational failure handling. Revisit trade-offs if requirements change. A good LLD answer is scoped, testable, and extensible.
cleanVoiceoverScript:
Interview Goal Design clean classes, interfaces, object flows, edge cases, tests, and trade-offs for Design a Parking Lot.

Problem Framing Model Design a Parking Lot with clear domain entities. Support support multiple parking floors and support different vehicle types. Keep the design extensible for new rules, states, and workflows. State scope, assumptions, and key workflows before drawing classes.

Functional Requirements Support multiple parking floors Support different vehicle types Assign nearest available parking spot Generate parking ticket Calculate parking fee on exit Requirements become methods, state transitions, and tests.

Constraints Keep the core workflow deterministic and testable. Keep state changes explicit and easy to validate. Design should support new rules without rewriting existing classes. Constraints decide whether the design needs locks, strategies, or repositories.

Core Objects Vehicle: Base class for all vehicles Car: Represents a car vehicle ParkingFloor: Manages parking spots on one floor ParkingSpot: Represents one parking slot ParkingTicket: Stores parking session details ParkingLot: Main coordinator for parking and exit operations Good LLD separates object responsibilities before adding methods.

Class Diagram ParkingLot coordinates the main workflow. Vehicle anchors the model. Composition owns object lifetime; dependencies express collaboration. Long explanations stay in the panel so the diagram remains clean. Use the diagram to prove ownership and collaboration, not to dump text.

Interface Contracts FeeCalculationStrategy: Calculates parking fee using pluggable pricing rules Use interfaces only where behavior genuinely varies.

Public Methods Vehicle.getVehicleType(): Base class for all vehicles Vehicle.getVehicleNumber(): Base class for all vehicles ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle): Manages parking spots on one floor ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle): Represents one parking slot ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle): Represents one parking slot Method contracts turn requirements into testable behavior.

Park Vehicle Flow Driver calls ParkingLot.parkVehicle(car) ParkingLot calls ParkingFloor.findAvailableSpot(vehicle) ParkingFloor calls ParkingSpot.canFit(vehicle) ParkingSpot calls ParkingSpot.parkVehicle(vehicle) ParkingLot calls ParkingTicket.createTicket(vehicle, spot) A collaboration flow proves that the class model is executable.

Exit Vehicle Flow Driver exits using the parking ticket. Parking lot calculates the fee using the selected fee strategy. The occupied spot is freed after payment. Sequence diagrams expose missing dependencies and invalid state changes.

State Machine SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved: SpotAvailable -> SpotReserved on entry. SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied: SpotReserved -> SpotOccupied after vehicle parks. SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable: SpotOccupied -> SpotAvailable after paid exit. Explicit states prevent invalid operations from slipping into code.

Singleton Used in ParkingLot Only one central parking lot manager should coordinate all parking operations. Name a pattern only when it solves a concrete design pressure.

Data Structures Map floorId to ParkingFloor. Priority queue or ordered list for nearest available spots. Map ticketId to active ParkingTicket. Data structures should justify time complexity and state ownership.

Edge Cases Reject unsupported input before mutating state. Validate availability before assigning resources. Validate state before allowing an operation. Edge cases reveal whether the design is robust or just happy-path.

Error Handling Return domain-specific errors for invalid state. Release temporary resources on failure. Keep failure messages actionable for callers. Handle failure before mutating shared or persistent state.

Concurrency Lock spot assignment per floor. Prevent two gates from reserving the same spot. Make ticket creation idempotent for retries. Interviewers often push LLD designs with concurrent requests.

Code Skeleton Show the core fields and method contracts. Keep behavior near the object that owns the state. Avoid turning the coordinator into a catch-all class. Code skeletons should prove design shape, not become full production code.

Tests as Design Proof Happy path creates the expected state changes. Invalid input is rejected without mutation. Concurrent requests do not allocate the same resource twice. Tests prove the class responsibilities and edge cases are clear.

Trade-offs Simple manager class vs multiple services Nearest spot allocation vs first available spot Fixed fee calculation vs strategy-based fee calculation In-memory design vs persistent storage Strong candidates explain why they did not over-engineer.

Mistakes to Avoid Putting all logic inside one ParkingLot class Ignoring vehicle type compatibility Not freeing the spot on exit Skipping sequence flow explanation Calling out pitfalls shows you understand failure modes.

Final Summary ParkingLot coordinates core use cases. Vehicle, Car, ParkingFloor carry primary state. Park Vehicle Flow explains object collaboration. Edge cases, tests, and trade-offs make the design interview-ready. End by showing how the design can evolve without changing everything.

Next Steps Add persistence and external API boundaries. Add metrics and operational failure handling. Revisit trade-offs if requirements change. A good LLD answer is scoped, testable, and extensible.
